padded_coif.name: padded coif
padded_coif.lore: A close-fitting cap of quilted linen stuffed with sheep's wool, designed to cushion the skull without impeding movement. Favored by those who prefer swift thinking over heavy protection — it offers little defense but weighs almost nothing.

leather_cap.name: leather cap
leather_cap.lore: A close-cropped leather cap, hardened with oil and shaped on a wooden form. Popular among scouts and rogues, it keeps the hair back without clanking and provides a genuine measure of protection against glancing blows.

iron_helm.name: iron helm
iron_helm.lore: A simple iron pot helm with a fixed nasal guard and cheek plates. The design dates back centuries and has never been improved because it has never needed to be — it protects what matters and costs little enough to leave behind if you must run.

chain_coif.name: chain coif
chain_coif.lore: Thousands of hand-riveted rings form this close-fitting hood, which conforms perfectly to the skull and neck. The chain drape is uncomfortable in sustained wearing but provides excellent protection against slashing attacks to the head and throat.

steel_helm.name: steel helm
steel_helm.lore: A segmented steel spangenhelm with a riveted brow band and hinged cheek guards. The polished surface deflects glancing blows with impressive efficiency, and the thoughtful ventilation prevents the overheating that plagues lesser designs.

great_helm.name: great helm
great_helm.lore: The full-face steel great helm encloses the entire head in a shell of tempered metal, with only a narrow vision slit revealing the wearer's eyes. Pierce attacks find no purchase on its seamless exterior; the trade-off is severely restricted peripheral vision and hearing.

plate_helm.name: plate helm
plate_helm.lore: A master armourer's plate helm with a hinged visor and articulated neck guard. The curved surfaces are calculated to deflect sword blows in any direction, and the padding within is shaped memory-leather that conforms to a specific wearer's skull.

crystal_helm.name: crystal helm
crystal_helm.lore: Faceted from resonant spell-crystal over twelve months of careful work, this helm intercepts magical waves before they reach the mind within. Enchanters who observe it note that spells cast at the wearer seem to slide off its angled faces like water.

dragonscale_helm.name: dragonscale helm
dragonscale_helm.lore: Constructed from the head-plates of a fire wyrm, this helm carries a vestigial draconic resistance to flame — fire seems simply not to reach the face of whoever wears it. The scales shift between gold and amber as the light changes, beautiful and deadly in equal measure.

adamantine_helm.name: adamantine helm
adamantine_helm.lore: The adamantine full helm is among the most protective headgear ever devised. Blunt impacts that would cave ordinary plate simply transfer their force sideways along the impossibly dense metal, leaving the wearer with a ringing head but an intact skull.

cloth_armor.name: cloth armor
cloth_armor.lore: A thick-woven garment of sturdy cloth offering minimal protection but maximum freedom of movement. Scholars and mages favor it for exactly this reason — their safety comes from knowing what's coming, not from what they're wearing when it arrives.

padded_armor.name: padded armor
padded_armor.lore: Layers of linen stuffed with wool and stitched into a fitted body garment, this armor is the first step beyond bare skin. Soldiers in poorer armies wear nothing else, and it turns aside more attacks than one might expect simply through its bulk and give.

leather_armor.name: leather armor
leather_armor.lore: Vegetable-tanned leather hardened in sections and fitted to a standard body form. The supple material moves with the wearer rather than against them, making this the most popular choice among those who need protection without sacrificing agility.

ring_mail.name: ring mail
ring_mail.lore: Iron rings punched through a leather backing in overlapping rows, heavier than simple leather but significantly more protective. The distinctive clicking sound of ring mail has warned many a monster that a well-armed opponent approaches.

scale_mail.name: scale mail
scale_mail.lore: Overlapping steel scales riveted to a leather backing, mimicking the natural armor of reptiles. The clever overlapping design causes slashing weapons to glance sideways along the scales rather than finding purchase, much to the frustration of would-be assassins.

chain_mail.name: chain mail
chain_mail.lore: Thousands of individually riveted iron rings interlocked in a four-in-one pattern, this hauberk is the backbone of professional soldiers' equipment. Both slashing and piercing weapons struggle to find a gap in the continuously flexible surface.

banded_mail.name: banded mail
banded_mail.lore: Horizontal bands of articulated steel plate sewn over a mail foundation, providing considerably more rigid protection while retaining some flexibility. Elite infantry favor it as the best compromise between armor weight and combat endurance.

splint_mail.name: splint mail
splint_mail.lore: Vertical steel splints lashed tightly between layers of heavy leather create a rigid body protection that defies blunt impact far better than chain. Warriors who fight giants and trolls swear by it — the vertical arrangement spreads impact force efficiently across the whole garment.

half_plate.name: half plate
half_plate.lore: Shaped steel plate covering the torso, shoulders, and upper arms, worn over a chain shirt for gap coverage. Knights errant who cannot afford full plate often settle for this, finding it provides extraordinary protection where the body is most vulnerable.

full_plate.name: full plate
full_plate.lore: The pinnacle of conventional armorsmithing — every plate fitted specifically to a single wearer's body, every joint articulated for full combat mobility. A full suit represents months of a master smith's work and costs more than most adventurers earn in a year.

crystal_plate.name: crystal plate
crystal_plate.lore: Grown rather than forged, this armor was cultivated from a single crystal seed over decades in a magically controlled environment. The resulting plates are lighter than steel yet harder, and the lattice structure dissipates both fire and cold energy with remarkable efficiency.

dragonscale_armor.name: dragonscale armor
dragonscale_armor.lore: Harvested from a dragon of ancient lineage, each scale on this armor is broader than a human palm and harder than forged steel. The draconic essence still saturates the scales, granting the wearer dramatic resistance to fire and acid — the dragon's own weapons turned to defense. This is one of the three pieces of the Dragonslayer's Set. When worn alongside the Dragonbone Longsword and the Dragonslayer's Ring, the wearer gains immunity to fear and +2 to all attacks against dragons.

adamantine_armor.name: adamantine armor
adamantine_armor.lore: The heaviest wearable armor in existence, forged from the most dense metal known to mortalkind. Both slashing and blunt attacks reduce to a fraction of their normal effect against adamantine — the metal simply refuses to deform. Wearing it requires exceptional strength and constitution, but those who do approach invulnerability against physical assault.

leather_bracers.name: leather bracers
leather_bracers.lore: Simple oil-hardened leather strapped over the forearms, protecting the vulnerable tendons and veins from glancing cuts. Archers prize them additionally for the bowstring snap they prevent — a small consideration that saved many an arm over years of practice.

iron_bracers.name: iron bracers
iron_bracers.lore: Riveted iron plates encasing the forearm from wrist to elbow. Soldiers wear these when expecting to block weapon strikes with their arms — a maneuver discouraged but sometimes unavoidable in close quarters where shield positioning breaks down.

steel_bracers.name: steel bracers
steel_bracers.lore: Articulated steel plates covering the forearm and upper wrist, hinged at the elbow for mobility. The flared wrist guard is designed to catch and redirect blade strikes, turning parries into counterattack opportunities for a skilled fighter.

plate_bracers.name: plate bracers
plate_bracers.lore: Full-coverage plate vambraces from a master armourer's workshop, shaped to follow the arm's musculature precisely. The curved surfaces deflect slashing attacks with geometric precision, and the articulated elbow cowter allows full arm extension without binding.

adamantine_bracers.name: adamantine bracers
adamantine_bracers.lore: Adamantine vambraces so dense that blocking a maul strike with them is effectively the same as blocking with a wall. Fighters wearing these will sometimes deliberately absorb blows to the forearms that would have been problematic to parry, exploiting the metal's almost supernatural durability.

cloth_gloves.name: cloth gloves
cloth_gloves.lore: Knitted from tight-spun wool, these gloves offer almost no physical protection but keep the hands warm and improve grip in damp dungeon passages. Scholars wear them to protect sensitive fingertips during extended reading sessions underground.

leather_gloves.name: leather gloves
leather_gloves.lore: Soft calfskin gloves fitted to exact finger measurements, providing excellent tactile feel and modest protection for the hands. Thieves prize gloves of this quality because they permit lock manipulation without leaving fingerprints — a practical consideration in any profession involving entry without invitation.

chain_gauntlets.name: chain gauntlets
chain_gauntlets.lore: Chain link gauntlets with a leather palm lining for grip, covering the hands and extending partway up the forearm. The flexible construction preserves weapon-handling dexterity while protecting the fingers and knuckles from cut damage.

steel_gauntlets.name: steel gauntlets
steel_gauntlets.lore: Articulated steel gauntlets with segmented finger plates, allowing full grip while protecting the back of the hand completely. Experienced fighters note that the hardened knuckles make unarmed strikes considerably more painful — an unintended but appreciated bonus.

plate_gauntlets.name: plate gauntlets
plate_gauntlets.lore: Plate gauntlets with fully articulated finger protection and reinforced knuckle guards that distribute blunt impact across the entire hand structure. Blacksmiths who work near forges have been known to covet them simply as heat protection — though their martial applications are considerably more dramatic.

adamantine_gauntlets.name: adamantine gauntlets
adamantine_gauntlets.lore: Gauntlets of pure adamantine, each finger plate a masterwork of segmented engineering. The metal is so dense that a punch from these gauntlets strikes with the force of a hammer blow; wearing them requires adjusting one's grip on weapons, as the enhanced weight changes every swing's momentum.

leather_leggings.name: leather leggings
leather_leggings.lore: Boiled leather shaped into fitted leg protection, covering thigh to knee in a single articulated piece. Scouts favor these over heavier alternatives because they protect the legs without the weight penalty that turns a sprint into a jog.

chain_leggings.name: chain leggings
chain_leggings.lore: Chain mail chausses covering leg and thigh, traditionally paired with a full hauberk for complete coverage. The flexible construction allows a full range of kicks and strides while turning aside most cutting attacks to the lower body.

steel_greaves.name: steel greaves
steel_greaves.lore: Articulated steel greaves covering shin and knee with a hinged poleyn for joint mobility. Infantry commanders discovered that unprotected legs were the primary target of low combat strikes, and the adoption of these greaves dramatically reduced debilitating lower-body wounds.

plate_greaves.name: plate greaves
plate_greaves.lore: Complete plate protection from hip to ankle, with articulated cuisses, poleyns, and greaves working in mechanical harmony. The engineering required to make these move with a wearer without binding represents a century of accumulated armorsmithing refinement.

adamantine_greaves.name: adamantine greaves
adamantine_greaves.lore: Adamantine greaves with a polished-dark surface that seems to absorb light. The material's extreme hardness means slashing attacks aimed at the legs — a classic strategy against armored opponents — simply fail to penetrate, leaving the wearer free to ignore the lower-body strikes most warriors rely upon.

leather_boots.name: leather boots
leather_boots.lore: Hard-soled boots of decent leather, worn smooth at the heel from long travel. They protect the feet from dungeon debris and the constant moisture of stone passages, while the flexible uppers allow silent movement when circumstances demand it.

iron_boots.name: iron boots
iron_boots.lore: Iron sabatons with articulated toe plates and a riveted ankle guard. The heavy iron construction means underground creatures who try to attack the feet find the experience as productive as attacking a stone floor — though the weight makes running feel like wading through mud.

steel_boots.name: steel boots
steel_boots.lore: Articulated steel sabatons shaped to follow the foot's natural flex, with reinforced toe caps that have broken more than a few monster ribs when their wearers chose to kick rather than step back. The polished steel exterior is a point of professional pride for every armourer who produces them.

plate_boots.name: plate boots
plate_boots.lore: Full-coverage plate boots from ankle to toe, with a complex mechanism of overlapping plates that somehow allows normal walking. Worn with full plate, they complete a suit of protection that covers every inch of the body in solid metal.

adamantine_boots.name: adamantine boots
adamantine_boots.lore: Adamantine boots so dense that every step leaves faint impressions in stone floors. Floor-level attacks — the trademark of dungeon ambushers who attack from below — find no purchase whatsoever, and the sound of these boots approaching has been known to send intelligent monsters fleeing without combat.

cloth_cloak.name: cloth cloak
cloth_cloak.lore: A wide, rough-woven woolen cloak that offers little protection but significant warmth. In the cold stone passages of deep dungeons, many adventurers have decided that comfort matters more than combat statistics — these people often survive longer than their more heavily armored companions.

leather_cloak.name: leather cloak
leather_cloak.lore: Water-proofed leather, cut wide and hung to the heel, with an inner layer of dense wool. The material turns aside glancing blows that would have opened cuts on an unprotected back, and the dark color makes its wearer less visible in shadowed corridors.

ring_cloak.name: ring cloak
ring_cloak.lore: A cape fashioned from interlocking iron rings, heavier than it looks and surprisingly protective against attacks from behind. Cavalry officers pioneered this design to guard their back and flanks during mounted combat; it translated well to dungeon fighting where rearward threats are constant.

crystal_cloak.name: crystal cloak
crystal_cloak.lore: Woven from spell-crystal filaments as fine as spider silk, this cloak shimmers like running water in firelight. The crystal lattice is naturally hostile to directed magical energy — spells that strike it lose coherence, dissipating harmlessly before they can affect the wearer.

dragonscale_cloak.name: dragonscale cloak
dragonscale_cloak.lore: This sleek cloak is fashioned from the wing membrane and smaller scales of a shadow-drake, a draconic subspecies known for their natural stealth and venomous bite. The scales absorb fire and seem to drink in ambient light, making the wearer unusually difficult to spot in dim conditions. This is one of the three pieces of the Shadow Walker's Set. When worn alongside the Adamantine Dagger and the Shadow Walker's Ring, the wearer gains a poison strike on all attacks and moves silently regardless of armor worn.

cloth_shirt.name: cloth shirt
cloth_shirt.lore: A plain linen undershirt, worn beneath armor to prevent chafing and absorb sweat. Adventurers who skip this basic layer quickly develop the kind of skin problems that make donning heavy armor a daily ordeal — comfort matters more than newcomers suspect.

padded_shirt.name: padded shirt
padded_shirt.lore: A fitted gambeson of quilted canvas stuffed with raw wool, worn as both a standalone garment and as padding beneath heavier armor. The thick quilting distributes impact force across a wide area, making it surprisingly effective against blunt weapons.

chain_shirt.name: chain shirt
chain_shirt.lore: A fine mesh of riveted rings worn beneath outer armor to protect gaps and joints. The shirt's true value is revealed when a blade finds the seam in a breastplate — instead of drawing blood, it meets the chain and glances away, foiled by thousands of interlocking circles of steel.

mithril_shirt.name: mithril shirt
mithril_shirt.lore: Lighter than silk, stronger than iron — the legendary mithril shirt has been sought by every warrior who ever heard of it. The silvery rings are impervious to corrosion and naturally hostile to magical energy, deflecting enchantments that would otherwise pass through conventional armor unimpeded.

adamantine_shirt.name: adamantine shirt
adamantine_shirt.lore: Adamantine rings fine enough to pass as chain mail, yet possessing all the hardness of the parent metal. Even worn alone it provides extraordinary protection against cutting weapons — slashing attacks that would split lesser mail simply fail to sever the adamantine links, regardless of force applied.

cloth_hood.name: cloth hood
cloth_hood.lore: A simple hood of rough-woven cloth. It keeps the dust out of your eyes and little else. Every adventurer starts somewhere.

bronze_helm.name: bronze helm
bronze_helm.lore: Bronze predates iron in human warfare by millennia. A well-cast bronze helmet provides surprising protection; Corinthian bronze helms offered full-face coverage and were worn by Greek hoplites for three centuries.

sallet.name: sallet
sallet.lore: The sallet was the standard helmet of 15th-century European infantry and cavalry. Its swept-back brim deflected downward sword cuts; its open face allowed clear vision. English archers at Agincourt wore sallets. It represents the high point of practical medieval helmet design.

bascinet.name: bascinet
bascinet.lore: The bascinet evolved from the simple iron skullcap into a sophisticated piece of armor with an articulated visor. Knights of the 14th century attached chain aventails to the bascinet's edges, protecting the neck and cheeks. The pointed top deflects downward blows laterally.

armet.name: armet
armet.lore: The armet was the pinnacle of 15th and 16th-century helmet craft: a fully enclosing helm that locked around the wearer's head for tournament and field use alike. The hinged cheekpieces created a sealed environment that was both uncomfortably warm and extraordinarily protective.

mithril_helm.name: mithril helm
mithril_helm.lore: Mithril, the moonmetal, weighs a third of steel yet provides equivalent protection through an enchantment woven into its crystalline structure. A mithril helm offers a subtle resistance to magical attacks — the metal refracts hostile spells the same way it refracts light.

brigandine.name: brigandine
brigandine.unidentified_name: a cloth coat with metal rivets
brigandine.lore: The brigandine was the armor of choice for 14th-century mercenary companies: small iron or steel plates riveted to a fabric outer coat, usually velvet or canvas. It combined the flexibility of cloth with reasonable protection and was far cheaper than a full coat of mail.

hauberk.name: hauberk
hauberk.lore: The hauberk was the primary body defense of Norman and crusading knights. A full hauberk extended to mid-thigh with skirts split for riding, long sleeves ending in mail mittens, and an attached coif. William the Conqueror's knights wore hauberks at Hastings in 1066.

coat_of_plates.name: coat of plates
coat_of_plates.lore: Transitional armor of the early 14th century: large overlapping iron plates riveted to a heavy fabric foundation. The Black Prince wore a coat of plates over his mail at Crécy. It represents the step between chain and full plate — more protection but heavier and less mobile than later plate armor.

mithril_plate.name: mithril plate
mithril_plate.lore: Mithril plate is the culmination of elven craft and dwarven metallurgy. It weighs half what steel plate does and deflects magical energies with unusual efficiency. A mithril plate suit was reportedly worn by a human wizard who survived a dragon's full breath weapon — though he described the experience as 'thoroughly unpleasant.'

cloth_wraps.name: cloth arm wraps
cloth_wraps.lore: Simple strips of linen wrapped around the forearms. They protect against minor abrasions and cold rather than serious weapons.

padded_bracers.name: padded bracers
padded_bracers.lore: Quilted linen bracers filled with wool provided the common soldier's minimal forearm protection. More importantly, they prevented the abrasion from a mail coat's interior surface.

bronze_bracers.name: bronze bracers
bronze_bracers.lore: Bronze greaves and bracers preceded iron equivalents in Greek and Roman armies. A pair of Mycenaean bronze bracers found at Dendra represents over 3,000 years of continuous use of this design.

chain_bracers.name: chain bracers
chain_bracers.lore: Chain mail bracers protect the forearms while allowing the hand full articulation. They are heavier than leather but significantly better at deflecting cuts.

scale_bracers.name: scale bracers
scale_bracers.lore: Overlapping scales of metal riveted to leather backing create excellent glancing resistance. Scale armor was standard issue in the armies of ancient Egypt and Persia.

crystal_bracers.name: crystal bracers
crystal_bracers.lore: Crystal bracers are grown rather than forged — mage-cultivated mineral formations shaped into armor. They resonate with defensive wards that reflect minor magical attacks harmlessly.

dragonscale_bracers.name: dragonscale bracers
dragonscale_bracers.lore: Dragonscale bracers require scales from the foreleg — the most densely armored section of any dragon. The fire resistance they provide is inherent to the scale's structure, not an enchantment.

iron_gauntlets.name: iron gauntlets
iron_gauntlets.lore: Early iron gauntlets were crude affairs: mitten-shaped iron coverings attached to mail sleeves. By the 13th century, articulated fingers had been developed. These iron examples represent the transitional form.

bronze_gauntlets.name: bronze gauntlets
bronze_gauntlets.lore: Bronze gauntlets were worn by Macedonian cavalry to protect sword hands. Their archaeological record dates to 700 BC. These examples follow the simple mitten form that was practical for three millennia.

mithril_gauntlets.name: mithril gauntlets
mithril_gauntlets.lore: Mithril gauntlets are fully articulated at every finger joint and provide complete protection without any loss of weapon control. Master craftspeople take six months to produce a single pair.

cloth_leggings.name: cloth leggings
cloth_leggings.lore: Simple cloth leggings provide no real protection but at least insulate against cold stone floors.

padded_leggings.name: padded leggings
padded_leggings.lore: Padded leggings were worn under heavier armor throughout the medieval period. They also served as the standalone leg protection of poorer soldiers who could not afford mail.

iron_leggings.name: iron leggings
iron_leggings.lore: Iron plate leg armor protected the vulnerable femoral artery from cutting weapons. Medieval surgeons noted that wounds to the upper leg were among the most dangerous — which explains why legionnaires spent so much on this protection.

scale_greaves.name: scale greaves
scale_greaves.lore: Scale greaves represent the Eastern approach to leg protection: flexible scales that move with the leg while providing good overall coverage. Byzantine cavalry used this pattern throughout the eastern empire.

crystal_greaves.name: crystal greaves
crystal_greaves.lore: Crystal greaves hum faintly when approached by hostile magic, providing both warning and protection. The resonance crystals in the knee joints can detect arcane energies up to 10 feet away.

cloth_sandals.name: cloth sandals
cloth_sandals.lore: The simplest footwear imaginable — straps of cloth wrapped around the foot and ankle. Offers no real protection but allows silent movement and is better than going barefoot on dungeon stone.

padded_boots.name: padded boots
padded_boots.lore: Padded boots filled with wool provide warmth and limited protection. They are the everyday footwear of merchants, farmers, and beginning adventurers.

bronze_boots.name: bronze boots
bronze_boots.lore: Bronze greaves covered the shins and were held in place by the metal's natural spring. Greek hoplites wore bronze greaves without straps; the metal gripped the leg by itself. These boots extend the protection to the foot.

chain_boots.name: chain boots
chain_boots.lore: Chain mail feet protection is more common than it might seem — crusading knights wore chain chaussures covering the entire foot in linked rings. They provided excellent protection against slashing while maintaining reasonable flexibility.

crystal_boots.name: crystal boots
crystal_boots.lore: Crystal boots grip the floor with surprising stability — the crystalline structure creates a natural adhesion that prevents slipping on wet stone. The magical resistance embedded in each facet extends from toe to calf.

mithril_boots.name: mithril boots
mithril_boots.lore: Mithril boots are so light that wearers report feeling faster — a subjective effect that tests have confirmed is objectively true. The metal's enchantments reduce impact, allowing quieter movement than any other protective footwear.

fur_cloak.name: fur cloak
fur_cloak.lore: A bearskin or wolf-pelt cloak was the mark of a northern warrior — practical insulation against the cold and an intimidating appearance in southern eyes. Several Viking chieftains were described wearing such cloaks over their mail.

wool_cloak.name: wool cloak
wool_cloak.lore: Undyed wool cloaks were the standard traveling garment across the medieval world. The natural lanolin in wool provides mild water resistance; the thickness provides warmth. Not impressive, but reliable.

silk_cloak.name: silk cloak
silk_cloak.lore: Silk is surprisingly effective at stopping arrows — the fiber wraps around arrowheads and prevents penetration. Mongol warriors wore silk undergarments precisely because removal of the garment could draw out arrowheads without surgery. A silk cloak extends this protection.

mithril_cloak.name: mithril cloak
mithril_cloak.lore: A mithril-woven cloak is the ultimate magical protection garment. The metal threads form an interference pattern that disrupts incoming spells. Arch-mages who have studied this effect describe it as 'the magic equivalent of signal jamming.'

shadowweave_cloak.name: shadowweave cloak
shadowweave_cloak.unidentified_name: a cloak that seems to absorb light
shadowweave_cloak.lore: Woven from adamantine threads thinner than silk, the shadowweave cloak is barely visible in dim light — it bends shadows around the wearer. Assassins covet these garments; rulers fear them. The cursed variants permanently darken the wearer's reputation in ways that are difficult to explain to historians.

linen_shirt.name: linen shirt
linen_shirt.lore: Worn against the skin beneath any other armor. Keeps sweat from rusting the mail and distributes the weight more comfortably. Everyone wears one.

wool_shirt.name: wool shirt
wool_shirt.lore: Thick wool against the skin in cold dungeons is not luxury — it is survival. Medieval knights wore wool garments under their mail in winter. The padding also reduces the transfer of impact forces from the outer armor.

silk_shirt.name: silk shirt
silk_shirt.lore: Silk's arrow-stopping properties were well known in the medieval east. A tight-woven silk undergarment catches and wraps arrowheads, preventing deeper penetration. Temur's cavalry reportedly wore silk shirts under their armor for exactly this reason.

scale_shirt.name: scale shirt
scale_shirt.lore: A scale shirt worn beneath outer armor is an old layering technique. The scales provide a rigid base that distributes the force of impacts, while the outer layer provides coverage. Egyptian and Mesopotamian warriors wore this combination for centuries.

elven_shirt.name: elven shirt
elven_shirt.lore: Elven craftspeople weave mithril into garments so fine they appear to be fabric rather than armor. An elven shirt can be folded into a package the size of a handkerchief. It weighs nothing and stops almost everything. Those few who have worn one describe feeling simultaneously naked and invincible.

hide_of_nemean_lion.name: Hide of the Nemean Lion
hide_of_nemean_lion.unidentified_name: a rough amber pelt of unnatural density
hide_of_nemean_lion.lore: The Nemean Lion's hide could not be cut by any weapon — that was the point of the first Labour. Heracles strangled it. The lion's own claws were the only things sharp enough to skin it. He wore the hide thereafter. Every subsequent monster he faced had to find a way to harm a man armoured in the unskinnable. Most didn't manage it. The hide renders its wearer virtually invulnerable to conventional weapons — only magical or divine arms can pierce it.

carapace_of_the_hydra.name: Carapace of the Hydra
carapace_of_the_hydra.unidentified_name: a greenish scaled vest that smells faintly of venom
carapace_of_the_hydra.lore: Heracles used the Hydra's caustic blood to poison his arrows — the same arrows that killed Chiron by accident and Nessus by intention and started the chain that killed Heracles himself. The Hydra's outer scale was immune to its own venom. This is the standard configuration: weapons made from the thing they destroy are often proof against the same.

panoply_of_hephaestus.name: Panoply of Hephaestus
panoply_of_hephaestus.unidentified_name: a suit of armour of inhuman precision
panoply_of_hephaestus.lore: Hephaestus was thrown from Olympus by his mother (or his father, depending on which Homer you read) and landed on Lemnos. He survived, became the smith of the gods, and made the best things in existence: Achilles' armour, the golden automata that walked him to his forge, the chain-net that caught Aphrodite and Ares in bed, the bronze giant Talos. Everything he made was perfect. He himself walked with a limp.

dragon_mail_of_sigurd.name: Dragon-Sewn Mail of Sigurd
dragon_mail_of_sigurd.unidentified_name: a shirt of scale-linked rings with a faint iridescence
dragon_mail_of_sigurd.lore: After killing Fafnir, Sigurd bathed in the dragon's blood and became invulnerable — except for a leaf that fell between his shoulder blades. His eventual murder came from a spear through exactly that spot, thrown by a man told about it by a woman who loved him and was told by another man who needed Sigurd dead. The weak point was the information, not the armour.

breastplate_of_joan.name: Breastplate of Joan of Arc
breastplate_of_joan.lore: Joan of Arc described her armour as 'very good' — white-painted steel made specifically for her. She was wounded multiple times and fought on. She told her judges at her trial that her armour had been made by command of God and could not protect against what God willed. They burned her anyway. Twenty-five years later Charles VII had the verdict annulled. Joan had been dead for twenty-five years.

lorica_hamata_of_caesar.name: Lorica of Caesar
lorica_hamata_of_caesar.unidentified_name: a fine ring-mail shirt of late Republican make
lorica_hamata_of_caesar.lore: Julius Caesar wore chain-mail. He also reportedly wore a special hat to cover his baldness and was sensitive about being called a king by the Senate. He was stabbed twenty-three times. The physician Antistius found only one wound fatal. The other senators had been imprecise or hesitant. Caesar had refused his bodyguard. He had been warned. He understood warnings as challenges.

orichalcum_breastplate.name: Orichalcum Breastplate
orichalcum_breastplate.unidentified_name: a chest-piece of reddish-gold metal that vibrates faintly
orichalcum_breastplate.lore: Orichalcum was the metal of Atlantis — mentioned by Plato in the Critias as the most prized substance in the world after gold. Its appearance was flame-red. The Atlanteans mined it everywhere, then it vanished when Atlantis sank. Modern scholars identify it as brass or a copper-zinc alloy. The identification is probably correct. Plato's text does not describe its resonance, which the surviving piece still exhibits.

helm_of_hades.name: Helm of Darkness
helm_of_hades.unidentified_name: a helm that seems to absorb light around it
helm_of_hades.lore: The Helm of Hades was made by the Cyclopes in the same forging that made Poseidon's trident and Zeus's lightning. Perseus borrowed it to approach the Gorgons unseen. Hermes lent it to Athena during the Trojan War. It did not grant invisibility — it prevented perception. The distinction seems technical until you realize the Helm worked on gods. No one can look away from nothing.

helm_of_achilles.name: Helm of Achilles
helm_of_achilles.unidentified_name: a bronze helm with a tall horse-hair crest
helm_of_achilles.lore: Hephaestus forged Achilles' new armour when Patroclus died wearing the old set. The helmet had a crest of horse-hair and rang like a bell. Achilles wore it for the last weeks of his life. It was awarded after his death to Odysseus over Ajax — a decision based on rhetoric and political judgement, not martial merit. The armour was better than both arguments.

great_helm_of_galahad.name: Great Helm of Galahad
great_helm_of_galahad.unidentified_name: a white helm with a red cross on the cheek
great_helm_of_galahad.lore: Galahad arrived at Camelot with a plain white shield and no name. He sat in the Siege Perilous — the seat that killed anyone not pure enough — and nothing happened. He was the only knight who completed the Grail quest because he was the only one who had not already compromised himself. His father Lancelot came within inches of it. Lancelot's single lapse in virtue cost him the quest and gave the world Galahad. The Grail works through irony.

vambraces_of_achilles.name: Vambraces of Achilles
vambraces_of_achilles.lore: Part of the panoply Hephaestus forged. The vambraces bore scenes of working men and women — farmers, shepherds, dancers — as though the smith god remembered that war is fought over ordinary lives. The warriors who wore them over centuries may not have understood the pictures. The pictures were not for them.

gauntlets_of_mars.name: Gauntlets of Mars
gauntlets_of_mars.lore: Mars was the second most worshipped god in Rome after Jupiter. Unlike Ares, his Greek equivalent, he was not merely the god of war but of agriculture and the protection of boundaries — the army that defends fields is the same army that works them. His priests carried sacred shields believed to have fallen from heaven and danced with them in procession every March. The month is named for him. So is Tuesday, through Tiwaz/Tyr.

greaves_of_hermes.name: Greaves of Hermes
greaves_of_hermes.unidentified_name: winged bronze greaves of incredible lightness
greaves_of_hermes.lore: Hermes' winged sandals — the Talaria — were given him by his father Zeus or possibly won in trade with Perseus or possibly always his. He carried the dead to Hades in them. Perseus borrowed them for the Medusa mission along with the Helm and Kibisis bag. The wings are the most depicted element of Hermes iconography, which is interesting because Hermes himself is most notable for what he carries between places, not the speed.

cloak_of_the_morrigan.name: Cloak of the Morrigan
cloak_of_the_morrigan.unidentified_name: a dark feathered cloak that moves without wind
cloak_of_the_morrigan.lore: The Morrigan — the Phantom Queen — appeared as a crow or raven on the battlefield and chose who lived and died. She approached Cu Chulainn three times in disguise: as a young woman, an eel, a wolf, and a heifer. He rejected and fought each of them. Before his final battle she appeared as an old woman milking a lame cow, and he drank from her and was healed without knowing it. He died anyway. She had been kind to him; he had not recognized it.

leggings_of_enkidu.name: Leggings of Enkidu
leggings_of_enkidu.unidentified_name: rough-worked leather greaves from before metallurgy
leggings_of_enkidu.lore: Enkidu was created by the gods as a wild man to counter Gilgamesh. He lived among animals until a temple prostitute civilised him over seven days. He then challenged Gilgamesh to single combat; they fought to a draw and became friends. The Epic of Gilgamesh is the oldest known written epic. Its central subject is not the monsters they killed together but what Gilgamesh does when Enkidu dies first.

mantle_of_elijah.name: Mantle of Elijah
mantle_of_elijah.unidentified_name: a rough prophetic cloak of undyed wool
mantle_of_elijah.lore: Elijah's mantle passed to Elisha when Elijah was taken to heaven in a chariot of fire. Elisha used it to part the Jordan River on his return. In Jewish tradition it represents the transfer of prophetic authority — the mantle is the office, not the man. When Elijah threw it on Elisha's shoulders without asking, Elisha left his oxen and ran after him. He was ploughing when it happened. He finished the field first.

robe_of_the_magus.name: Robe of the Magus
robe_of_the_magus.unidentified_name: a deep blue robe with silver astronomical diagrams
robe_of_the_magus.lore: The Magi of the ancient Near East were not magicians in the popular sense — they were scholars of astronomy, dream interpretation, and the hidden correspondences between celestial and earthly events. The Star they followed was either a conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn, a comet, or a nova. They brought gold, frankincense, and myrrh and went home 'another way,' having been warned in a dream not to report back to Herod.

boots_of_seven_leagues.name: Boots of Seven Leagues
boots_of_seven_leagues.unidentified_name: a pair of worn leather boots that feel ready to move
boots_of_seven_leagues.lore: In European folklore, seven-league boots take their wearer seven leagues — about 35 km — per step. Hop-o'-My-Thumb stole them from an ogre. Puss in Boots wore them. They appear across French, German, and Scandinavian tales with minor variation: the boots are always disproportionately large on their original owner and always fit the hero perfectly. The folklore does not explain the sizing mechanism.

brigandine_of_william_wallace.name: Brigandine of Wallace
brigandine_of_william_wallace.unidentified_name: a riveted coat of small iron plates over leather
brigandine_of_william_wallace.lore: William Wallace was not the painted barbarian of popular cinema. He was a second son of a minor noble family who became a guerrilla commander, won the Battle of Stirling Bridge by understanding terrain better than the English generals, was eventually captured, and was executed by a method the English used specifically to maximise dishonour. He gave no speech at his death. He denied nothing. The armour is real; the speeches were invented later.

cuirass_of_hannibal.name: Cuirass of Hannibal
cuirass_of_hannibal.lore: Hannibal Barca crossed the Alps with war elephants in 218 BCE and fought Rome for sixteen years on its own soil. He won every major engagement: Trebia, Trasimene, Cannae — Cannae being the most studied battle in military history, used as a model for encirclement tactics by commanders for two millennia. He never took Rome. He was eventually called home to defend Carthage, lost one battle, and died in exile. He won the tactics and lost the war. Military academies still teach him.

nemean_pelt.name: Nemean Lion Pelt
nemean_pelt.lore: The hide of the Nemean Lion, impossible to cut or pierce. Hercules used its own claws to skin it. Wearing it is like wearing the beast itself.

green_knights_plate.name: Green Knight's Plate
green_knights_plate.lore: Forged from enchanted iron that grows with the wearer, the Green Knight's Plate absorbs blows that would kill a lesser man. The wearer heals slowly but continuously.

serpent_scale_mail.name: Serpent Scale Mail
serpent_scale_mail.lore: Scales from Jörmungandr's juvenile form, each harder than iron. The poison that suffuses them has been neutralized — but it still repels toxins entirely.

arachne_silk_cloak.name: Arachne's Silk Cloak
arachne_silk_cloak.lore: Woven by Arachne before her transformation, this cloak is lighter than air and tougher than leather against piercing weapons. It catches arrows and slows blades.

erlking_mantle.name: Erlking's Mantle
erlking_mantle.lore: Woven from the living branches of the Erlking's forest, this cloak sharpens the senses of the wearer. Sound and sight carry further, and hidden things become easier to discern.

anansi_web_cloak.name: Anansi's Web Cloak
anansi_web_cloak.lore: Spun by Anansi himself from the thread of a thousand stories, this cloak shifts and deceives. Attacks that should land somehow miss. Traps somehow fail to trigger.

nidhoggr_scale.name: Níðhöggr's Scale
nidhoggr_scale.lore: A single scale from Níðhöggr's body, imbued with the rot of ages. Nothing corrodes it. Wearing it grants the wearer immunity to acid and strong resistance to all decay-based attacks.

aegishjalmr.name: Aegishjalmr
aegishjalmr.unidentified_name: a helm inscribed with a terrifying rune
aegishjalmr.lore: The Helm of Awe, ægishjálmr, was worn by the dragon Fafnir. All who looked upon it were struck with dread. The Volsunga saga records that Sigurd took it from the dragon's hoard, and with it gained the power to terrify armies with a glance.

winged_sandals_of_hermes.name: Winged Sandals of Hermes
winged_sandals_of_hermes.unidentified_name: sandals with small golden wings at the ankles
winged_sandals_of_hermes.lore: The talaria of Hermes, messenger of the gods, allowed him to fly between Olympus, Earth, and the Underworld in moments. Perseus borrowed them to approach Medusa. These sandals carry their wearer with divine swiftness — each step covers ground that would take mortals three.

hermes_sandals_early.name: Hermes's Sandals
hermes_sandals_early.unidentified_name: a pair of sandals with tiny wing motifs
hermes_sandals_early.lore: A mortal imitation of Hermes's divine talaria. The wings are decorative, but the sandals carry a fragment of the messenger god's speed. They will not carry you between worlds, but they will carry you faster than your enemies expect.

blindfold.name: blindfold
blindfold.lore: A strip of heavy black cloth. Wearing it plunges you into total darkness — you cannot see anything at all. A desperate measure, but sometimes not seeing is the only way to survive what lurks in the dark.

vidars_sandal.name: Vidar's Sandal
vidars_sandal.lore: A sandal of impossible thickness, reinforced with leather scraps collected over an entire lifetime. Vidar the Silent, son of Odin, wore it when he avenged his father — planting his foot on the lower jaw of the World-Wolf and tearing the beast apart with his bare hands.

trainers_cap.name: Trainer's Cap
trainers_cap.lore: A well-worn red and white cap, its brim shaped by countless adventures. It radiates a warm, encouraging energy that invigorates companion creatures, helping them recover from wounds more quickly. The inside of the brim reads: 'I choose you.'

cow_kings_horns.name: Cow King's Horns
cow_kings_horns.lore: The gilded horns of the Cow King, ripped from his crowned skull by a victorious adventurer. When worn, the horns channel bovine fury into every strike, granting a free chain hit before the quiz even begins — making it impossible to miss entirely. The left horn is inscribed with a single word: 'Moo.'

cloth_of_penelope.name: Cloth of Penelope
cloth_of_penelope.lore: Penelope, wife of Odysseus, waited twenty years for her husband's return from Troy. Each day she wove a funeral shroud; each night she unraveled it, buying time against the suitors who demanded she remarry. This cloth is woven from that same thread — thread that refused to stay finished, that healed itself every night. When worn, the wearer's wounds close slowly but surely, as if time itself is being unwoven.

bracers_of_cu_chulainn.name: Bracers of Cu Chulainn
bracers_of_cu_chulainn.lore: Cu Chulainn, the Hound of Ulster, was Ireland's greatest warrior — a man who entered such battle frenzy that his body twisted and contorted, one eye bulging, his hair standing like spikes of flame. These bracers are stained with the blood of a hundred battles. They carry the warp-spasm's echo: the wearer strikes harder, bleeds enemies with every blow, and feels no pain from cuts.

cloak_of_sun_wukong.name: Cloak of Sun Wukong
cloak_of_sun_wukong.lore: The Monkey King, Sun Wukong, was born from a stone egg on the Mountain of Flowers and Fruit. He stole the peaches of immortality, defied the Jade Emperor, and was imprisoned under a mountain for five hundred years before accompanying the monk Tripitaka westward. His seventy-two transformations made him impossible to pin down. This cloak carries that trickster spirit — the wearer seems to shimmer and shift, making them difficult to strike.
